diagnosis: Peptic ulcer disease with upper GI bleed
accepted_answers: PUD bleed; bleeding peptic ulcer; duodenal ulcer bleed; gastric ulcer bleed
clue_1: A 58-year-old with osteoarthritis has two days of black stools and lightheadedness.
clue_2: He has vague epigastric burning that improves briefly after meals.
clue_3: Home medications include daily naproxen and aspirin after a prior TIA.
clue_4: His BUN is disproportionately elevated compared with creatinine.
clue_5: Nasogastric lavage returns coffee-ground material.
clue_6: He has no stigmata of chronic liver disease and platelets are normal.
explanation: Bleeding peptic ulcer disease is the most common upper GI bleed source and is supported by H. pylori or NSAID risk, epigastric pain, melena, and an elevated BUN:Cr ratio from digested blood protein.
high_yield: H. pylori/NSAIDs; epigastric pain; melena or coffee-ground emesis; BUN:Cr >20; endoscopy diagnostic and therapeutic.

accepted_answers: variceal bleed; bleeding esophageal varices; portal hypertensive varices
clue_1: A 54-year-old with alcohol-related cirrhosis suddenly vomits a large volume of bright red blood.
clue_2: He denies abdominal pain before the bleeding began.
clue_3: Exam shows ascites, spider angiomas, palmar erythema, and splenomegaly.
clue_4: Platelets are 68,000 and INR is elevated.
clue_5: Albumin is low and bilirubin is mildly increased.
clue_6: The bleeding is brisk enough to cause orthostasis.
explanation: Portal hypertension dilates the esophageal venous plexus, and rupture causes painless, often massive hematemesis in a patient with cirrhosis and coagulopathy.
high_yield: Cirrhosis/portal HTN; painless large-volume hematemesis; thrombocytopenia; elevated INR; highest-mortality UGIB pattern.

accepted_answers: Mallory Weiss tear; GE junction mucosal tear; post-retching hematemesis
clue_1: A 29-year-old has several hours of nonbloody vomiting after binge drinking.
clue_2: After repeated retching, the emesis becomes streaked with bright red blood.
clue_3: He has mild epigastric tenderness but no peritoneal signs.
clue_4: Hemoglobin is only slightly decreased.
clue_5: Liver tests and platelet count are normal.
clue_6: Endoscopy would show a linear mucosal laceration at the gastroesophageal junction.
explanation: A Mallory-Weiss tear is a longitudinal mucosal tear at the gastroesophageal junction caused by sudden increases in intraabdominal pressure from retching.
high_yield: Vomiting first, then hematemesis; GE junction linear tear; alcoholism, bulimia, hyperemesis; usually self-limited.

accepted_answers: cholecystitis; acute calculous cholecystitis; cystic duct obstruction
clue_1: A 42-year-old woman has constant RUQ pain for 10 hours after a fatty meal.
clue_2: The pain radiates to the right scapula and is associated with fever and vomiting.
clue_3: She previously had brief postprandial RUQ attacks that resolved spontaneously.
clue_4: She stops inhaling when the examiner palpates under the right costal margin.
clue_5: Ultrasound shows gallstones, wall thickening, and pericholecystic fluid.
clue_6: Direct bilirubin is only mildly elevated.
explanation: Acute cholecystitis is inflammation from cystic duct obstruction, producing constant RUQ pain, fever, Murphy sign, and gallbladder inflammatory changes on ultrasound.
high_yield: Constant RUQ pain; right shoulder/scapula radiation; fever; Murphy sign; gallstones + wall thickening/pericholecystic fluid.

accepted_answers: cholangitis; acute ascending cholangitis; infected choledocholithiasis
clue_1: A 67-year-old with known gallstones presents with RUQ pain, fever, and jaundice.
clue_2: He becomes confused and hypotensive in the emergency department.
clue_3: Labs show direct hyperbilirubinemia with ALP and GGT much higher than AST/ALT.
clue_4: Ultrasound shows a dilated common bile duct.
clue_5: Lipase is mildly elevated but does not explain the sepsis.
clue_6: Urgent ERCP is planned after resuscitation and antibiotics.
explanation: Ascending cholangitis occurs when an obstructed biliary system becomes infected, causing RUQ pain, fever, jaundice, and sometimes shock or altered mental status.
high_yield: Charcot triad = fever + RUQ pain + jaundice; Reynold pentad adds hypotension and AMS; ALP/GGT/direct bilirubin high; dilated CBD; ERCP treats.

accepted_answers: acute hepatocellular hepatitis; acute liver injury; acute viral hepatitis pattern
clue_1: A 24-year-old develops malaise, anorexia, nausea, and RUQ heaviness after travel.
clue_2: Several days later she notices dark urine, jaundice, and clay-colored stools.
clue_3: AST and ALT are both above 1000.
clue_4: Alkaline phosphatase is only modestly elevated.
clue_5: Ultrasound shows no common bile duct dilation.
clue_6: Serologies identify an acute viral exposure.
explanation: Acute hepatitis causes hepatocellular injury with prodrome, jaundice, RUQ discomfort, and very high transaminases, often far exceeding the cholestatic enzymes.
high_yield: Viral/drug/toxic exposure; malaise and anorexia; AST/ALT often >1000; jaundice/dark urine; rule out obstruction.

accepted_answers: perforated ulcer; perforated duodenal ulcer; perforated gastric ulcer
clue_1: A 61-year-old taking chronic NSAIDs develops sudden excruciating epigastric pain while at dinner.
clue_2: Pain rapidly becomes diffuse and he lies still with shallow breathing.
clue_3: The abdomen is board-like with rebound and guarding.
clue_4: Bowel sounds are absent.
clue_5: Upright chest radiograph shows free air under the diaphragm.
clue_6: Serum amylase is mildly elevated, but lipase is not diagnostic for pancreatitis.
explanation: Full-thickness ulcer perforation spills gastric or duodenal contents into the peritoneum, causing sudden severe pain, rigid abdomen, peritonitis, and free intraperitoneal air.
high_yield: Sudden maximal pain; rigid abdomen; peritonitis; H. pylori/NSAIDs; free air under diaphragm = perforation.

accepted_answers: GERD; reflux esophagitis; erosive esophagitis
clue_1: A 39-year-old with obesity has burning substernal discomfort after late meals.
clue_2: Symptoms worsen when lying flat and improve with antacids.
clue_3: He wakes with sour regurgitation and a chronic dry cough.
clue_4: Cardiac evaluation is unrevealing.
clue_5: He has no weight loss, anemia, dysphagia, or GI bleeding.
clue_6: An empiric proton pump inhibitor trial is started.
explanation: GERD results from lower esophageal sphincter dysfunction with acid reflux, producing postprandial and positional burning pain with regurgitation.
high_yield: Burning substernal/epigastric pain; worse after meals or lying flat; sour regurgitation; antacid/PPI response; alarm symptoms need endoscopy.

accepted_answers: pancreatitis; gallstone pancreatitis; alcoholic pancreatitis
clue_1: A 45-year-old develops severe epigastric pain after a weekend of heavy alcohol use.
clue_2: The pain radiates straight through to the back and is worse supine.
clue_3: He leans forward over the bed for partial relief.
clue_4: Vomiting does not improve the pain.
clue_5: Lipase is more than three times the upper limit of normal.
clue_6: Bowel sounds are decreased and there is voluntary guarding.
explanation: Acute pancreatitis is pancreatic enzyme activation causing severe epigastric pain radiating to the back, relief leaning forward, and lipase elevation above three times normal.
high_yield: Epigastric pain to back; better leaning forward; lipase >3x ULN; gallstones/alcohol; ALT >150 suggests gallstone cause.

accepted_answers: acute gastritis; chronic gastritis; H pylori gastritis; autoimmune gastritis
clue_1: A 53-year-old has dull epigastric burning, nausea, bloating, and early satiety.
clue_2: He uses ibuprofen most days but denies melena or weight loss.
clue_3: Endoscopy shows erythematous gastric mucosa without a discrete ulcer crater.
clue_4: Biopsy is positive on rapid urease testing.
clue_5: Hemoglobin is normal and BUN:Cr is not elevated.
clue_6: Symptoms improve after H. pylori eradication and stopping NSAIDs.
explanation: Gastritis is gastric mucosal inflammation without a discrete ulcer; H. pylori, NSAIDs, alcohol, and autoimmune parietal cell destruction are common causes.
high_yield: Epigastric discomfort/nausea; no discrete ulcer on endoscopy; H. pylori/NSAIDs; autoimmune gastritis causes B12 deficiency, achlorhydria, high gastrin.

accepted_answers: appendicitis; inflamed appendix
clue_1: A 19-year-old develops vague periumbilical pain that later localizes to the RLQ.
clue_2: Anorexia began before the pain became severe.
clue_3: Vomiting occurs after the pain, not before it.

clue_5: WBC count is elevated with a left shift.
clue_6: CT shows an enlarged blind-ending tubular structure with periappendiceal fat stranding.
explanation: Appendiceal lumen obstruction causes distension and inflammation, classically producing periumbilical pain that migrates to the RLQ with anorexia and leukocytosis.
high_yield: Periumbilical to RLQ migration; anorexia; pain before vomiting; McBurney/Rovsing/psoas/obturator signs; CT gold standard in nonpregnant adults.

accepted_answers: Crohn; Crohn's disease; regional enteritis; transmural IBD
clue_1: A 27-year-old smoker has months of crampy RLQ pain, low-grade fevers, and weight loss.
clue_2: Diarrhea is usually nonbloody.
clue_3: Exam reveals perianal skin tags and a draining fistula.

clue_5: Colonoscopy shows skip lesions with cobblestoning and rectal sparing.
clue_6: Biopsy reveals noncaseating granulomas.
explanation: Crohn disease is transmural, skip-lesion inflammatory bowel disease that commonly involves the terminal ileum and causes fistulas, strictures, perianal disease, and nonbloody diarrhea.
high_yield: Transmural; skip lesions; terminal ileum/RLQ; fistulas/strictures/perianal disease; ASCA; smoking worsens; B12 deficiency possible.

accepted_answers: hemolysis; hemolytic jaundice; prehepatic jaundice
clue_1: A patient presents with jaundice, pallor, fatigue, and splenomegaly but no RUQ pain.
clue_2: Bilirubin is predominantly unconjugated.
clue_3: LDH is high and haptoglobin is low.
clue_4: Reticulocyte count is elevated.
clue_5: Urinalysis lacks bilirubin despite dark urine from excess urobilinogen.
clue_6: Peripheral smear shows spherocytes.
explanation: Hemolysis overwhelms hepatic conjugation capacity, causing unconjugated hyperbilirubinemia with elevated LDH, low haptoglobin, and reticulocytosis.
high_yield: Indirect bilirubin; high LDH; low haptoglobin; high reticulocytes; no bilirubinuria; smear and Coombs help identify cause.

accepted_answers: Gilbert; Gilbert disease; benign unconjugated hyperbilirubinemia
clue_1: A healthy college student notices mild scleral icterus during final exams and fasting.

clue_3: AST, ALT, ALP, and CBC are normal.
clue_4: LDH, haptoglobin, and reticulocyte count do not suggest hemolysis.
clue_5: Symptoms resolve after normal eating resumes.
clue_6: Family history reveals similar benign episodes.
explanation: Gilbert syndrome is benign reduced bilirubin conjugation causing intermittent isolated unconjugated hyperbilirubinemia triggered by fasting, illness, stress, or exertion.
high_yield: Mild isolated indirect bilirubin; normal CBC and LFTs; fasting/stress/illness trigger; benign UGT1A1 deficiency.

accepted_answers: alcohol-associated hepatitis; alcoholic steatohepatitis
clue_1: A 49-year-old with heavy alcohol use develops jaundice, fever, anorexia, and RUQ pain.

high_yield: Heavy alcohol use; jaundice; tender hepatomegaly; AST:ALT >2:1; AST rarely >400; elevated GGT/INR; Maddrey DF for severity.

accepted_answers: common bile duct stone; biliary obstruction from gallstone; obstructive choledocholithiasis
clue_1: A patient has episodic RUQ pain followed by jaundice and dark urine.

clue_6: There is no hypotension or rigors to suggest infected obstruction.
explanation: Choledocholithiasis is a gallstone obstructing the common bile duct, producing conjugated hyperbilirubinemia, cholestatic enzymes, dark urine, pale stools, and ductal dilation.
high_yield: RUQ pain + obstructive labs; direct bilirubin and ALP/GGT high; dilated CBD; MRCP/EUS visualizes; ERCP treats.

accepted_answers: pancreatic head adenocarcinoma; pancreatic head malignancy; periampullary pancreatic cancer

clue_6: He recently developed diabetes without weight gain.
explanation: A pancreatic head mass compresses the distal bile duct, causing painless progressive obstructive jaundice, weight loss, Courvoisier sign, and the double-duct sign.
high_yield: Painless progressive jaundice; weight loss; Courvoisier sign; double duct sign; smoking/chronic pancreatitis/diabetes risk; CA 19-9 not diagnostic alone.

accepted_answers: ascites due to cirrhosis; portal hypertensive ascites; decompensated cirrhosis
clue_1: A patient with chronic hepatitis C has progressive abdominal distension and ankle edema.
clue_2: Exam shows spider angiomas, palmar erythema, splenomegaly, and bulging flanks.
clue_3: Diagnostic paracentesis shows SAAG of 1.5.

high_yield: SAAG >=1.1 indicates portal hypertension; cirrhotic ascites has low protein; always tap new/worsening ascites to exclude SBP.

accepted_answers: SBP; infected cirrhotic ascites; spontaneous peritonitis

explanation: SBP is monomicrobial infection of cirrhotic ascites due to bacterial translocation, diagnosed by ascitic PMNs over 250 cells/mm3.
high_yield: Cirrhotic ascites + fever/pain/encephalopathy; PMN >250 = treat; low-protein ascites risk; distinguish from secondary peritonitis.

accepted_answers: diverticular hemorrhage; bleeding colonic diverticulosis

explanation: Diverticular bleeding is arterial rupture of vasa recta at diverticula, causing sudden painless large-volume hematochezia in older adults.
high_yield: Most common significant adult LGIB; painless massive hematochezia; soft nontender abdomen; colonoscopy/CTA/tagged RBC depending stability.

accepted_answers: colon cancer; rectal cancer; colorectal adenocarcinoma; CRC

accepted_answers: colonic ischemia; ischemic colopathy

high_yield: Pain before bleeding; elderly vascular patient; watershed areas; thumbprinting; segmental mucosal injury; lactate/peritonitis suggests severe ischemia.

accepted_answers: UC; ulcerative proctocolitis; mucosal inflammatory bowel disease

accepted_answers: hemorrhoids; bleeding internal hemorrhoids; painless hemorrhoidal bleeding

accepted_answers: SBO; adhesive small bowel obstruction; mechanical small bowel obstruction

accepted_answers: LBO; colonic obstruction; obstructing colon cancer

accepted_answers: adult sigmoid volvulus; sigmoid colon volvulus

accepted_answers: celiac sprue; gluten-sensitive enteropathy; gluten enteropathy

accepted_answers: midgut volvulus; malrotation volvulus; neonatal volvulus
